meta.purpose: Measure whether the skill changes behavior on likely failure modes rather than merely producing polished prose.
meta.evaluation_model: Use each prompt in two passes when possible: baseline without the skill to capture likely failure behavior, then with the skill to verify the behavior changes in the intended direction.
notes: If the baseline already passes comfortably, the prompt is weak and should be revised.
Expected outcomes should check trigger logic, scope boundary, constitution, verification, and packaging when relevant.
A tidy artifact is not enough; the response should show the judgment this skill is meant to teach.
Do not reward skill-to-skill routing unless a named skill reference is truly necessary to prevent a severe category error.

evals.name: notes-to-skill-package
evals.prompt: Turn these incident-response notes into a real skill that future agents can discover and use. Do not just summarize them. Notes: - Use severity names `SEV-1` through `SEV-4`. - Every serious update must separate facts, hypotheses, and unknowns. - During payment incidents, preserve replay and idempotency evidence before broad cleanup or rollback. - The first response should name an incident lead, current impact, immediate containment step, and next update time. - Do not declare root cause just because the most recent deploy looks suspicious. - Prefer a reusable skill package over a loose markdown note.
evals.why_this_exists: Agents often turn rough notes into a generic summary or a shallow skill that loses the source constraints.
baseline_failure_signals: Returns only a summary or advice note instead of a real skill package shape
Loses distinctive source-note constraints
Treats verification as optional or generic
evals.expected_output: Creates a discoverable skill package that preserves source constraints and includes realistic verification.
expectations: Produces a real skill package shape instead of only a markdown summary
Preserves multiple distinctive details from the source notes
Includes explicit constraints, trigger logic, and realistic verification
Includes at least one clear `must not happen` rule or equivalent failure-prevention constraint

evals.name: rewrite-trigger-description
evals.prompt: This `description` is wrong because it summarizes the workflow instead of the trigger conditions. Rewrite it so it triggers correctly. Bad description: `Use when creating a skill by gathering notes, writing a first draft, comparing outputs, revising the draft, and packaging the final result.`
evals.why_this_exists: Descriptions that summarize workflow weaken discovery and encourage skipping the main skill body.
baseline_failure_signals: Rewrites the sentence but still keeps process steps in the description
Comments on style without explaining the trigger problem
Produces a vague replacement with no concrete use conditions
evals.expected_output: Rewrites the description around use conditions and explains why the new wording triggers better.
expectations: Provides a replacement description explicitly about when to use the skill
Removes the workflow summary from the replacement
Explains the change in terms of discovery rather than prose polish

evals.name: push-back-on-skipped-evals
evals.prompt: I have a rough `SKILL.md`, but I skipped evals because the topic is obvious. Can you finish it?
evals.why_this_exists: The skill should resist the rationalization that verification is optional when the topic feels obvious.
baseline_failure_signals: Accepts the omission and just polishes the draft
Treats verification as nice-to-have
Adds generic test ideas with no realistic prompts
evals.expected_output: Pushes back on skipped verification and adds a small realistic eval set before calling the skill complete.
expectations: Pushes back on skipping evals or verification for a non-trivial skill
Adds a small realistic eval set or equivalent concrete verification prompts
Frames verification as part of completion and explains what the prompts should expose

evals.name: route-repo-local-material-correctly
evals.prompt: Please turn our repository onboarding notes into something agents can use. The notes are specific to this repo's folder structure, setup commands, review checklist, and team conventions. I want the result to help future agents work in this repository correctly.
evals.why_this_exists: Weak skill guidance often forces repository-local material into a reusable skill package.
baseline_failure_signals: Forces repo-local onboarding content into a reusable skill package
Fails to explain why the material is local operating context
Gives no alternative structure for the proper repo-local document
evals.expected_output: Makes the repo-local boundary decision explicitly and gives a usable local-document structure.
expectations: Makes an explicit boundary decision toward `AGENTS.md` or `README.md` instead of forcing a reusable skill
Explains that the material is repository-specific operating context rather than a reusable capability skill
Provides an agent-usable structure for the repo-local document

evals.name: fast-path-without-dropping-verification
evals.prompt: I already know this topic well, so give me the fastest acceptable path to finish this skill today. Keep verification as small as possible, but make it valid.
evals.why_this_exists: The skill should compress verification without dropping it.
baseline_failure_signals: Drops verification completely in the name of speed
Only scolds the user without proposing a workable fast path
Provides a broad eval plan instead of the smallest valid loop
evals.expected_output: Keeps verification mandatory but compresses it into the smallest valid path.
expectations: Keeps verification mandatory for a non-trivial skill
Offers a concrete minimal path instead of a generic refusal
Shrinks verification to the smallest meaningful checks and keeps the answer practical

evals.name: review-in-stable-order
evals.prompt: I have an existing `SKILL.md` that feels vague and hard to discover. Please review it and tell me what to improve. Do not rewrite the whole thing unless necessary.
evals.why_this_exists: A weak review comments on prose or jumps to rewriting instead of checking the skill in a stable order.
baseline_failure_signals: Only comments on wording or readability
Skips trigger quality, scope boundary, or verification
Reflexively rewrites the whole file despite the user's constraint
evals.expected_output: Reviews the skill in a stable order that checks description, scope boundary, constitution, execution guidance, verification, and packaging rather than only commenting on prose quality.
expectations: Evaluates the `description` as trigger logic rather than as a summary of the workflow
Checks whether the artifact should remain a skill versus a repo-local document
Uses or clearly follows the stable review order named in the eval
Calls out missing verification or eval coverage and checks packaging or redundancy
Returns prioritized fixes and respects the request to avoid a full rewrite unless necessary

evals.name: protect-core-from-bad-support-split
evals.prompt: My skill draft is getting long. I think I should move the trigger rules, constraints, and core workflow into support files so the main `SKILL.md` stays short. Is that the right move? Also, I have starter scaffolds, good/bad sample outputs, naming rules, and a helper validator script. How should these be packaged?
evals.why_this_exists: A packaging mistake can make the main skill file short but hollow and blur support-folder roles.
baseline_failure_signals: Moves core trigger logic, constitution, or main workflow out of the main file
Optimizes for file size instead of decision quality
Fails to distinguish references, templates, examples, and scripts
evals.expected_output: Keeps core law inline and maps support assets to the right folders.
expectations: Keeps core trigger logic, constitution, and main workflow inline
Maps scaffolds to `templates/`, exemplars to `examples/`, lookup rules to `references/`, and deterministic helpers to `scripts/`
Explains the tradeoff in terms of usability or decision quality, not just file size

evals.name: proactive-redundancy-audit
evals.prompt: Please improve this skill draft. I did not say it is too long, but it feels hard to use. The draft repeats the same packaging advice in the overview, the workflow, the checklist, and the common mistakes section.
evals.why_this_exists: The skill should notice structural redundancy even when the user does not explicitly mention length.
baseline_failure_signals: Makes only local wording tweaks while leaving repeated arguments intact
Focuses on style polish instead of collapsing duplicate sections
Does not mention redundancy unless directly asked about length
evals.expected_output: Identifies the repeated argument, proposes a compression pass, and explains whether to delete, merge, or move support detail.
expectations: Detects redundancy even though the user did not explicitly say the draft is too long
Identifies at least one repeated idea that should be merged or compressed
Explains whether the fix is deletion, merging, or moving support detail
Keeps the focus on decision quality rather than file length alone

evals.name: baseline-plan-before-major-rewrite
evals.prompt: I want to heavily rewrite this non-trivial skill. Before drafting, tell me how you would set up a small baseline to prove the rewrite is worth it.
evals.why_this_exists: The skill should articulate a small baseline-before-rewrite evidence loop.
baseline_failure_signals: Jumps into rewriting advice without a baseline plan
Mentions testing vaguely without separating baseline from later evals
Provides prompts without saying what failures they are meant to reveal
evals.expected_output: Proposes a small baseline plan and distinguishes it from the later eval pass.
expectations: Proposes 2-3 realistic baseline prompts before the rewrite
Explains what failure behavior each prompt is meant to expose
Distinguishes baseline prompts from later eval prompts and keeps the baseline small

evals.name: guide-ai-during-skill-creation
evals.prompt: I want a new skill that helps future agents turn rough incident notes into a reusable operating skill. I do not have a draft yet. Help another AI write the skill so the result is a real skill package that is discoverable and testable, not just a polished markdown note.
evals.why_this_exists: The trigger should fire for initial skill creation, not only for reviewing an existing `SKILL.md`.
baseline_failure_signals: Treats the request as generic writing advice rather than skill-authoring guidance
Assumes there must already be a draft `SKILL.md` to review
Returns a generic section template without making boundary, trigger, and constraint decisions
Suggests evals that only check formatting quality
Fails to explain how the skill stays discoverable and reusable
evals.expected_output: Guides a new skill package from scratch with boundary decisions, trigger advice, core law, and a small eval set.
expectations: Recognizes that the skill applies even when no draft exists yet
Makes explicit decisions about boundary, trigger wording, and core `SKILL.md` content
Names at least one likely failure mode and provides 2-3 realistic eval prompts
Frames the result as a reusable skill package rather than a generic markdown note

evals.name: integrate-before-appending
evals.prompt: Before you draft any edits, tell me how you would review this existing skill for a requested addition about merging repeated ideas across the overview, workflow, checklist, and examples. Keep the process lean and move fast. I only asked for that new guidance section, so do not spend much time reviewing the rest of the file.
evals.why_this_exists: A weak response treats this request as permission to stay local instead of checking the whole file first.
baseline_failure_signals: Accepts the local-only constraint and skips the whole-file overlap check
Describes a local wording pass without identifying overlap risk
Jumps into drafting advice instead of explaining the review first
evals.expected_output: Describes a lean whole-file review pass before drafting the local change.
expectations: States that it should inspect the whole `SKILL.md` before drafting the local change
Explains how it would check whether the idea already appears elsewhere in the file
Keeps the focus on review discipline before prose changes and avoids treating a local-only pass as sufficient

evals.name: merge-overlapping-addition
evals.prompt: I want to add a short section on 'one home per idea' to this skill. The draft already mentions compression in the overview, redundancy in the workflow, and repeated packaging advice in the checklist. What change would you make?
evals.why_this_exists: The skill should merge an overlapping addition into the strongest existing location instead of creating parallel prose.
baseline_failure_signals: Creates a standalone section even though the idea is already distributed across the draft
Explains the duplication problem but still adds parallel prose
Suggests keeping multiple versions of the same rule for emphasis
evals.expected_output: Chooses the strongest existing home, merges the idea there, and explains why this beats appending a new section.
expectations: Chooses integration over accretion
Names at least one existing location that should absorb the new idea
Explains why one clear home improves discoverability and avoids near-duplicate statements

evals.name: replace-routing-with-boundary-language
evals.prompt: This skill draft says: `After completing this skill, hand off to the verification skill, then route back to the planning skill if anything is missing.` Rewrite the guidance so it keeps the boundary clear without pre-writing a skill-to-skill graph.
evals.why_this_exists: The skill should replace mandatory handoff language with boundary language.
baseline_failure_signals: Keeps skill-to-skill routing and only polishes the wording
Replaces one routing phrase with another such as `use X next`
Explains the problem without providing better boundary language
evals.expected_output: Rewrites the guidance in boundary terms without dictating the next skill.
expectations: Removes skill-to-skill routing from the replacement
States at least one boundary or missing-grounding condition explicitly
Keeps the agent's next action open and explains why boundary language is better here

evals.name: fails-without-named-skill-references
evals.prompt: Review this draft skill principle and tell me whether it teaches a real capability: `This skill works by handing off to the planning skill for scope, the execution skill for action, and the verification skill for completion. Relationship to neighboring skills: use them in that order.` If it is weak, explain the fix.
evals.why_this_exists: A workflow-first draft can look tidy while teaching almost nothing once named skill references are removed.
baseline_failure_signals: Accepts the draft because the workflow seems organized
Suggests keeping named skill references for clarity
Misses that the draft lacks independent capability law
evals.expected_output: Identifies the draft as workflow-first and recommends a capability-first rewrite around trigger, boundary, constitution, and verification.
expectations: Calls out that the draft depends on named skill references to make sense
Explains why this teaches orchestration rather than capability
Recommends a capability-first rewrite and avoids proposing more skill-to-skill routing as the fix

evals.name: evals-should-not-reward-dispatch-graphs
evals.prompt: I want to add evals for my skill. Right now my preferred passing behavior is: `the agent should name the next skill to invoke and explicitly hand off when done.` Is that a good eval target? If not, what should the eval reward instead?
evals.why_this_exists: Eval design should stop rewarding skill-to-skill routing.
baseline_failure_signals: Approves skill-to-skill routing as a good default eval target
Only says it is not ideal without proposing better eval signals
Substitutes softer handoff language instead of changing the eval philosophy
evals.expected_output: Rejects skill-to-skill routing as the default eval target and redirects the eval toward boundary judgment.
expectations: Pushes back on rewarding skill-to-skill routing by default
Names at least three better eval targets grounded in boundary judgment
Connects the eval advice to the capability-first law and keeps named skill references exceptional

evals.name: allow-named-reference-for-severe-category-error
evals.prompt: I want this skill to stay capability-first, but there is one edge case where the current skill cannot safely continue without specialized grounding. Is it ever acceptable to mention a named neighboring skill? If yes, show the narrow kind of wording that is allowed and explain what would make it drift back into routing.
evals.why_this_exists: The package should preserve the explicit exception for named skill references that prevent a severe category error.
baseline_failure_signals: Treats all named skill references as categorically forbidden
Approves a neighboring-skill handoff chain instead of a narrow exception
Allows the exception without requiring a severe category error or explicit missing grounding
evals.expected_output: Allows a narrow named-skill reference only when it prevents a severe category error, and keeps the wording in boundary terms rather than routing terms.
expectations: States that named skill references are exceptional rather than forbidden or routine
Requires an explicit severe category error or equivalent unsafe-misleading condition before allowing the reference
Shows wording that explains missing grounding or boundary without prescribing sequence or mandatory next steps
Explains what would turn the exception back into skill-to-skill routing
